claim: Claude Code v2.1.81 added rate_limits fields (five_hour and seven_day) to the statusline stdin JSON schema, enabling native rate limit display without external API calls.
The mapleleafu/ccstatusline repo from Session #229 now returns 404. sirmalloc/ccstatusline (5.8k stars, v2.2.6) is the canonical successor or rename and is referenced in official Claude Code docs.

The Windows statusline regression (GitHub #31670) has a confirmed community workaround as of v2.1.81: create ~/.claude/.claude.json with workspace trust entries and use forward slashes with Node.js in the command path.

The fnm wrapper adds 100-300ms per statusline render by running shell initialization, fnm env evaluation, and fnm use before executing the actual script. Using a direct node path bypasses this entirely.
Claude Code's statusline debounce interval is 300ms with in-flight cancellation -- scripts taking longer than 300ms risk being cancelled before their output is rendered.
A Go binary statusline achieves 8-29ms total execution time vs 47-150ms for Node.js (direct) or 167-470ms for Node.js with fnm shim. This is a 5-16x performance improvement.

Nine distinct implementation approaches exist for Claude Code statuslines: Enhanced Monolith (JS), Adopt ccstatusline, Hybrid wrapper, Go binary, Rust binary, Bash+jq, Plugin marketplace, Starship/CShip, and PowerShell. Five were eliminated for performance or fit reasons.

The anomaly-driven visibility pattern (quiet when healthy, expanding when problems detected) is confirmed as the optimal design philosophy by cross-referencing 15+ tools: Starship, lualine, VS Code, JetBrains, Copilot, btop, and lazygit all implement variants of this pattern.

Predictive compaction (~N msgs left) is a unique feature with no equivalent in any third-party tool. It transforms the context bar from a passive gauge into an actionable countdown using linear extrapolation from pace tracking samples.

Seven cross-cutting design principles emerged from analyzing 15+ tools: color language consistency, 5-7 widget rule, healthy=quiet/unhealthy=loud, progressive disclosure by width, left=identity/right=metrics, symbolic density, and event-driven refresh.

The evil_lualine single-character colored bar (▊) provides instant peripheral-vision state awareness in exactly 1 character: green=healthy, yellow=warnings, red=critical, blue=working, magenta=agent mode.

VS Code's full-bar background color change (blue=workspace, orange=debugging, purple=no folder) is the most transferable pattern for Claude Code: the entire statusline background can signal overall system state without reading text.
The Copilot state machine iconography (3-state: working/ready/error) maps directly to Claude Code session states. A 5-state icon (idle/working/agent/permission/error) covers 95% of user needs.
PowerShell statusline scripts are unacceptable due to 200-500ms cold start overhead, which is worse than Node.js + fnm. This eliminates the PowerShell-native approach entirely.

No mature, cross-platform, Node.js-native tool exists that provides a persistent, live-updating status bar coexisting cleanly with an interactive shell session. This gap is what custom statusline solutions fill.
Terminal multiplexers (tmux, Zellij) provide the gold standard for status bars but require wrapping the shell session and have no native Windows support. Not viable for SoNash on Windows.

The VT100 scroll-region technique (used by Python bottombar) is the most portable approach for standalone status bars, but no Node.js implementation exists and it conflicts with programs that manipulate scroll regions.

Starship and Oh My Posh are prompt renderers, not status bars -- they update only at prompt time, not live between commands. They cannot serve as Claude Code statusline commands directly but can complement one.

The compositor/wrapper pattern (combining output from multiple statusline tools) produces the worst performance of any approach (195-395ms) because each tool spawns a sequential subprocess. Not viable.

All SoNash-specific widgets (debt ticker, hook health, health grade, GSD task tracking, predictive compaction, PR review state) require local filesystem reads. No third-party tool provides these. This is the fundamental reason to build custom rather than adopt.

Eight visual layout patterns were evaluated. The recommended design is Pattern E (anomaly-driven) with Pattern H (color-zone) accents and Pattern B (segmented) as the underlying architecture: quiet single line normally, expanding with color-highlighted anomaly segments when thresholds are crossed.

Width-aware progressive degradation should follow a priority order where the context percentage is the LAST widget to disappear and session timer is the FIRST to drop. Five tiers: >=120ch, >=100ch, >=80ch, >=60ch, <60ch.

The statusline snapshot write pattern (writing summary JSON to .claude/state/statusline-snapshot.json on every render) creates a compaction recovery anchor, session-start accelerator, and crash recovery signal at near-zero cost.

Go 1.23.6 is installed on this machine at /c/Users/jbell/go/bin/go, making a Go binary implementation feasible without additional toolchain setup. Cross-compilation to Windows is trivial (GOOS=windows GOARCH=amd64 go build).
The git dirty widget (git status --porcelain) costs 50-200ms and was correctly excluded from all implementation plans. It doubles the git cost and exceeds the performance budget as a single widget.

ccstatusline uses React + Ink for TUI rendering, adding 40-80ms warm execution overhead. Its npx invocation is particularly slow (500-2000ms due to npm registry check). A global install (80-150ms) is required for acceptable performance.

The Claude Code plugin marketplace (72+ plugins, 24 categories) is designed for reusable, project-agnostic tools. SoNash-specific statusline widgets are project-specific and not a good fit for the marketplace model.

JetBrains inspections widget pattern (checkmark when clean, warning triangle with count when warnings, red circle with count for errors) maps directly to the hook health widget design: green checkmark for passing, yellow triangle for warnings, red X for failures.

The Copilot CLI Status Sidebar Proposal (Issue #981) establishes an information hierarchy for AI tool status that applies to Claude Code: (1) context/tokens, (2) cost, (3) state/connectivity, (4) changed files. Statuslines should prioritize in this same order.

The issue #31670 Windows statusline regression was caused by two interacting failures in the Bun-based Windows native binary: an AbortError in child_process spawn and a silent workspace trust check that kills execution without visible error.
